Written Summary
Sentiment Analysis
How students felt during their conversations — based on explicit signals in student messages

Most conversations are task-focused

95.3% of conversations contain no explicit positive or negative signals. Students are asking questions and getting answers — this is normal for a support tool. Absence of frustration is itself a signal that the bot is not actively failing them.

Explicit satisfaction: 503 students

2.7% of students used language like "thank you", "that's helpful", "sorted", "perfect", "much appreciated". These are students who actively confirmed the bot helped.

104 students stuck without escalation

104 conversations show clear frustration signals — students asking to speak to a human — but the conversation never escalated. This is the direct footprint of CF-16 (loop behaviour). Still live post-fix.

Trajectory: 79 conversations got worse

Of conversations long enough to track trajectory, 79 showed worsening sentiment — students who started neutral and became frustrated. 35 improved. Most (1571) were stable throughout.

Failure Pattern Breakdown
Types of broken conversations detected this month
Topics & Escalation Rates
What students are coming for, and whether the bot resolves it or hands off to a human

Graduation & Attendance: bot adds friction

Graduation escalates at 72%, Attendance at 70%. The bot is processing these conversations but almost always ending in a human handoff. Students in these topics are being made to wait.

Technical issues: no escalation path

Technical/Access has 0% escalation but 11.7% negative sentiment — the highest of any topic. Students with login and access problems are getting bot responses that don't help, and there's no route to a human. This is a silent failure. This is a configuration gap, not a Learnwise limitation — a flow with a "Contact IT Support" button can be added without any engineering effort.

Finance: high volume, half always escalate

Finance is the second largest topic at 4,496 conversations with a 52% escalation rate. The bot is handling initial triage but most finance queries need human action (payments, invoices, refunds). Consider whether earlier routing saves effort.

Letters/ID: high volume, bot handles well

The biggest topic at 4,760 conversations, only 32% escalation and minimal negative sentiment. The bot is successfully self-serving the majority of letter and ID queries.

Daily Trends

Vertical line = 22 May (Dani's fixes deployed)
Pre / Post Fix Comparison
Dani's Learnwise fixes deployed ~09:27 on 22 May. Pre = 1–21 May (13,580 conversations). Post = 22 May – 1 Jun (5,059 conversations).

Ghost button: fully resolved

Explicit "can't find the button" complaints: 73 pre-fix → 1 post-fix. The global BPP instruction causing the phantom button reference has been removed. No residual cases in the post-fix window.

Loop behaviour: improved but not fixed

Loop conversations (frustrated, unescalated, 8+ messages): 0.7% → 0.3%. A meaningful drop, but CF-16 is still live. Students who want a human and can't get one are still present in the data.

Escalation rate: lower post-fix

Overall escalation rate dropped from 36.4% to 32.3% after the fixes. Part of this will be the loop fix reducing redundant escalation attempts; part may be seasonal.

Metric Pre-fix (1–21 May, n=13,580) Post-fix (22 May–1 Jun, n=5,059) Change
Escalation rate 36.4% 32.3% ↓ 4.1pp
Negative sentiment rate 2.1% 1.8% ↓ 0.3pp
Loop failure rate 0.7% 0.3% ↓ 0.4pp
Ghost button complaints 73 (0.5%) 1 (0.0%) ↓ 0.5pp

Written Summary & Recommendations
Analysis of 18,639 conversations, 1 May – 1 June 2026

CX Bot — May 2026 Performance Assessment

The headline

The bot handled 18,639 student conversations in May. 64.7% did not require escalation to a human agent — these were self-served entirely through the bot. 35.3% escalated, which means 1 in 3 conversations still required human involvement. The bot is handling volume, but there are clear topic areas where it is adding friction rather than removing it.

Where the bot is working

Letters & ID — 4,760 conversations, 32% escalation, minimal frustration

The bot's biggest topic and it handles it well. Students asking about enrolment letters, invitation letters, and ID documents are largely self-served. Real examples from May:

  • "I need invitation letter for my wife"
  • "Can you help me generate a letter?"

The bot successfully provides template links, explains turnaround times, and handles multiple letter types without needing a human. This represents the bot at its best — high volume, low escalation, low frustration.

Results & Grades — 889 conversations, 0% escalation

Students asking about results, transcripts, and grade timelines are almost never escalated. The bot surfaces grade release timelines, explains the marking process, and directs to relevant pages — students rarely need a human because the information exists and the bot finds it. Real examples:

  • "How to get provisional transcript. As I am in second semester now"
  • "I am going to India from 1–30 June and my student visa expires on 24 August — my result will be out on 11 June 2026 so when will BPP send my completion information to the Home Office"

Low negative sentiment (0.7%) confirms this is genuine resolution, not silent failure. The bot is actually useful here.

Ghost button: fixed (CF-18 — confirmed closed)

The phantom button issue has been resolved. 73 explicit "I can't find the button" complaints in the pre-fix window; zero in the 10 days since Dani's fix on 22 May. Root cause: a global BPP instruction told the bot to say "click the button below" when knowledge articles directed students to support — but no button was enabled. Instruction removed ~22 May. Confirmed closed.

Where the bot is struggling

The clearest failures are a missing escalation path for specific topics and a bot that loops rather than handing off to a human.

1. Technical / Access issues — highest frustration (11.7% neg), zero escalation

Students with login problems, access errors, and technical issues express the highest negative sentiment of any topic — but the bot never escalates them. They get generic suggestions (clear cache, try another browser), confirm they've already tried those, and then get the same suggestions again with no exit route. Real examples of what these students are dealing with:

  • "My BPP account has been blocked, and I want to reset my password — university sent me a link for reset but the link is not received"
  • "Where to login to apply for jobs — give me link"
  • "How do I access the library"

This is a configuration gap, not a Learnwise limitation. A flow with a "Contact IT Support" button that triggers on technical/access keywords can be added without any engineering effort.

2. Graduation (72% escalation) and Attendance (70% escalation) — bot adds steps before the inevitable

Nearly every conversation in these topics ends with a human. The bot processes the conversation but saves nobody time. What students are actually asking:

Graduation — students asking about guest tickets, ceremony details, gown hire, and specific ceremony dates:

  • "Can you confirm me when the convocation email is issued"
  • "Could you tell me when the date of my convocation — I study in MSc Healthcare Leadership 2025 in London BPP University"

Attendance — students disputing records or asking why they're marked absent, which always needs a caseworker with system access:

  • "I have scanned the QR but my name is not displayed when the tutor checks"
  • "How to raise a query for attendance"

Graduation queries mostly need a human to actually book or confirm something — the bot can provide information but can't action anything. Attendance disputes always need system access. Consider whether early routing to a human is better than a full bot conversation for both topics.

3. CF-16 loop — still live (104 frustrated-unescalated conversations), deadline 6 June

104 conversations show students explicitly asking for a human who never received escalation. The pattern: student says "I need to speak to someone", bot asks for more information, student repeats, bot asks again. Post-fix the loop rate dropped from 0.7% to 0.3%, but the root cause — the bot has no unconditional escalation path — has not been solved. The fix repositioned the loop flow but didn't add the fundamental "after N failed attempts, always escalate" logic. Deadline for Dani's rework: 6 June 2026. Retest owner: Gina Tomes.

4. Enrolment / Changes (6.4% neg) and Visa (6.5% neg) — high-stakes queries with elevated frustration

These are among the highest-stakes queries a student can have. What students are asking:

Enrolment — withdrawal, deferral, module changes:

  • "I want to withdraw from my programme and return back to my country Nepal — what should I have to do"
  • "How do I enrol to resit"

Visa — CAS letters, Home Office notifications, immigration questions:

  • "If I would like to apply for a graduate visa, how do I know whether BPP has told the Home Office I've successfully completed the course"
  • "Steps for visa process"

The bot gives generic information but can't access a student's actual enrolment record. For visa queries in particular, incomplete or wrong information has real consequences — consider escalation-first routing for all visa queries.

What the sentiment data actually tells us

95.3% of conversations contain no explicit emotional signal — students ask a question, get a response, and leave. This is expected and healthy for a support tool. The meaningful signal is in the explicit sentiment: 456 students (2.4%) actively confirmed the bot or human helped; 374 students (2.0%) expressed clear frustration.

Of the 456 conversations with explicit positive signals, 222 (1.2%) were in conversations that never escalated — these are genuinely bot-resolved. The remaining 234 came from conversations that did escalate, where the student's thanks likely reflects the human resolution, not the bot. The true bot-resolved positive signal is 1.2% of all conversations.

CSAT is available for only 163 conversations (0.9% response rate) — too small to be statistically reliable. Of those: 73.6% gave 5/5, 17.2% gave 1/5. The bimodal distribution is consistent with a bot that works well for straightforward queries and fails badly for complex or high-stakes ones.

Pre / post fix (22 May)

The Learnwise fixes on 22 May produced measurable improvement: escalation rate down 36.4% → 32.3%, negative sentiment down 2.1% → 1.8%, loops down 0.7% → 0.3%. Ghost button gone entirely. The fixes worked, but CF-16 remains the open structural problem — Dani reworking all flows, deadline 6 June.

Top 3 recommendations

R1 — Add an escalation path for Technical/Access queries (priority: high)

Students with login and access problems are expressing the most frustration and have nowhere to go. Add a flow with a "Contact IT Support" button that triggers on technical/access keywords. This is a Learnwise configuration change only — no engineering effort required. This is the single highest-impact change available right now given the combination of high frustration and zero escalation path.

R2 — Resolve CF-16 properly: unconditional escalation after N failed turns (priority: high)

The current fix repositioned the loop flow but didn't add the fundamental capability. The bot needs a rule: if a student has explicitly asked for a human 2+ times and has not been escalated, trigger escalation unconditionally. Deadline: 6 June 2026 (Dani/Learnwise). Acceptance criteria: Gina's 5 failing conversation IDs from 26 May must all pass before this is marked done.

R3 — Review Graduation and Attendance bot content (priority: medium)

At 70–72% escalation these topics are close to "always escalate" territory. Before investing in knowledge base improvements, validate whether the escalation is appropriate (the query genuinely needs human action) or friction-driven (bad answer forces escalation). If the former, consider early routing — fast-track to a human escalation option rather than making students go through a full bot conversation first.

What to tell Joe / Yani

The bot is self-serving 64.7% of all conversations without human involvement. Letters & ID (biggest topic) and Results & Grades are working well. The ghost button issue is resolved. The true bot-resolved positive signal is 1.2% (222 students) — the broader 2.4% figure includes students who thanked the human agent after escalation. The remaining open problems are CF-16 (students who ask for a human don't always get one, fix due 6 June) and the Technical/Access gap (11.7% frustration, no escalation route). Both are fixable with Learnwise configuration, not engineering effort.
